home.title: See how React really works
home.subtitle: Watch the JavaScript event loop tick, virtual DOM trees diff, keys match up and hooks fire — all as step-by-step animations you control.

eventLoop.hint: microtasks drain fully before each macrotask

event-loop.title: The JS Event Loop
event-loop.tagline: Call stack, Web APIs, macro & micro task queues — animated.
event-loop.intro: JavaScript runs on a single thread — it can only do one thing at a time. So how does it handle timers, network calls and promises without freezing? The answer is the event loop: a coordinator that shuffles work between the call stack, the browser's Web APIs, and two queues.
event-loop.ruleBody: When the call stack is empty, the event loop first empties the entire microtask queue (Promise callbacks), and only then takes one macrotask (a setTimeout/event callback) — after which it drains microtasks again. That's why the output above is 1 → 4 → 3 → 2 even though the timeout was set to 0 ms.
event-loop.whyBody: React batches state updates and flushes effects around these same queues. Understanding micro- vs macro-tasks explains why a setState inside a promise behaves differently from one inside a setTimeout, and why useLayoutEffect can run before the browser ever paints.

virtual-dom.title: Virtual DOM
virtual-dom.tagline: How JSX becomes a lightweight tree React can reason about.
virtual-dom.intro: When you write JSX, the browser never sees it. A compiler (Babel or the TypeScript/SWC toolchain) rewrites every tag into a React.createElement call, which returns an ordinary JavaScript object. Nest those objects and you get a tree — the Virtual DOM: a cheap, in-memory description of what the UI should look like.
steps: A component is just a function that returns JSX.
The compiler turns each JSX tag into a plain JS object node.
Children become nested objects — a whole tree in memory.
This lightweight object tree is the Virtual DOM. No real DOM yet!
virtual-dom.calloutTitle: Why keep a copy in memory?
virtual-dom.calloutBody: Touching the real DOM is slow. By building and comparing these lightweight objects first, React can figure out the smallest set of real DOM operations before it touches the page — which is exactly what the next lessons show.

reconciliation.title: Reconciliation & Diffing
reconciliation.tagline: Comparing two virtual trees to find the minimal change.
reconciliation.intro: On every render React produces a brand-new Virtual DOM tree. Instead of throwing away the page and rebuilding it, it diffs the new tree against the previous one. This comparison — called reconciliation — walks both trees together and records the minimal list of changes.
steps: React holds the previous tree and compares it against the freshly rendered one.
Compare the roots: <ul> vs <ul> — same type, so reuse the node.

Child 1:
  • Buy milk
    • is identical in both. Nothing to do.
Child 2: same <li>, but the text changed "dog" → "cat". Patch the text.

Child 3:
  • Read book
    • exists only in the new tree → insert it.
Done. From a whole re-render React derived just 1 text patch + 1 insert.
reconciliation.calloutBody: React's diff is O(n) because it assumes: (1) two elements of different types produce different trees (so it replaces the whole subtree), and (2) children can be matched across renders with a stable key — the subject of the next lesson.

keys.title: The Role of Keys
keys.tagline: Why keys make list diffing correct and cheap.
keys.intro1: When React diffs a list, it needs to know which new item corresponds to which old one. A key is that identity tag. Use the array index and inserting at the top shifts every item to a new index, so React thinks every row changed. Use a stable id and React matches rows correctly, touching only the one that actually moved.
keys.intro2: Click Add to top and watch how many DOM nodes each strategy has to update.
keys.calloutBody: Never use the array index as a key for a list that can reorder, filter or grow at the top. Reach for a stable, unique id from your data. Index keys are only safe for a static list that never changes order.

commit.title: Committing to the Real DOM
commit.tagline: Only the changed node is touched in the actual DOM.
commit.intro: Diffing happens in the render phase and produces a list of changes — but nothing is on screen yet. In the commit phase React applies exactly those changes to the real DOM, and only those. A one-character text change becomes a single textContent assignment — not a rebuild of the card.
steps: Starting point: the Virtual DOM and the real DOM agree. Count is 0.
setCount(1) runs. React re-renders the component into a new tree.

Diff (reconciliation): only the

text differs — everything else matches.
Commit: React runs ONE operation — p.textContent = "Count: 1".
Done. The <div>, <h1> and <button> DOM nodes were never touched.
commit.calloutBody: A component "re-rendering" only means React called your function and rebuilt a virtual tree. That's cheap. The expensive part — touching the DOM — is kept to the minimum diff. This is why unnecessary renders are usually fine, but not free (the next lessons show how to avoid them).

state-render.title: State → Re-render
state-render.tagline: How setState kicks off render, diff and commit.
state-render.intro: State is React's memory. When you call a state setter like setCount, you're not just changing a variable — you are scheduling a re-render. React then runs a four-stage pipeline to get that new state onto the screen.
state-render.calloutTitle: Renders are cheap; commits are targeted
state-render.calloutBody: The render counter climbs by exactly one per click — one setCount schedules one re-render of this component. (The four-stage animation runs inside a separate child component, so its own updates don't inflate the count.) Yet, as the previous lessons showed, React still only touches the single DOM text node that changed.

usestate-vs-useref.title: useState vs useRef
usestate-vs-useref.intro: Both useState and useRef let a component remember a value between renders. The difference is what happens when you change it: updating state re-renders the component, while mutating a ref does not. A ref is a plain, mutable box ({ current }) that survives renders without triggering them.
usestate-vs-useref.stateHint: ▲ updating this re-renders → the UI always matches
usestate-vs-useref.refHint: ▲ this value is live in memory, but the screen only catches up on the next render
usestate-vs-useref.calloutBody: Click refCount.current++ a few times — the big number won't move, because no render happened. Now click Force a re-render and the ref's real, accumulated value suddenly appears. The value was changing all along; React just had no reason to repaint.
usestate-vs-useref.whenTitle: When to reach for a ref
usestate-vs-useref.whenBody: Use a ref for values that shouldn't drive the UI: a DOM node (ref={inputRef}), a timer/interval id, the previous value of a prop, or any mutable bookkeeping. Use state whenever the value should be shown and kept in sync on screen.

usecallback.title: useCallback
usecallback.tagline: Keeping a function identity stable across renders.
usecallback.intro: Every render creates fresh function objects. Usually that's harmless — but if you pass a function as a prop to a React.memo child, a new function each render looks like a changed prop, so the child re-renders anyway. useCallback(fn, deps) hands you back the same function reference until a dependency changes.
usecallback.hint: Each click re-renders the parent. Watch which child follows along.
usecallback.callout1Body: The left child's render count climbs with every parent render — its function prop is different each time. The right child stays put: useCallback kept the reference identical, so React.memo could skip it.
usecallback.callout2Body: useCallback only pays off when the function is a dependency of something (a memo child, a useEffect, or another hook). Wrapping every handler adds memory and complexity for no benefit. Measure first.

usememo.title: useMemo
usememo.tagline: Caching an expensive computation between renders.
usememo.intro: Where useCallback caches a function, useMemo caches a value. If a computation is expensive, you don't want to redo it on every render — only when its inputs change. useMemo(() => compute(x), [x]) remembers the last result and returns it untouched until x changes.
usememo.withoutDesc: times the slow function ran — climbs on every render, even unrelated ones
usememo.calloutBody: Click Re-render (unrelated state) repeatedly: the left counter races ahead while the right one stays flat. Now change n — both tick up once, because the cached value's dependency actually changed.
usememo.vsBody: They're the same idea. In fact useCallback(fn, deps) is just useMemo(() => fn, deps). Use useMemo for computed data (a filtered list, a derived object) and useCallback for the function you'll pass down.

react-memo.title: React.memo
react-memo.tagline: Skip a child render when its props did not change.
react-memo.intro: By default, when a component re-renders, all of its children re-render too — even if their props are identical. React.memo(Component) wraps a component so React first does a shallow compare of its props; if nothing changed, it reuses the previous result and skips rendering that subtree.
react-memo.callout1Body:
Change unrelated parent state: the parent and the plain child re-render, but the memo child does not — its value prop is unchanged.
Change the child's prop: now value differs, so even the memo child re-renders. That's the point — it skips unnecessary renders, not necessary ones.
react-memo.callout2Body: React.memo compares props shallowly. If you pass a new object, array, or inline function each render, the memo "breaks" because the reference differs every time. That's exactly where useMemo and useCallback come in — they keep those references stable so memo can do its job.

effect-vs-layouteffect.title: useEffect vs useLayoutEffect
effect-vs-layouteffect.tagline: Timing relative to the browser paint.
effect-vs-layouteffect.intro: Both hooks run after render, but on opposite sides of the browser paint. useLayoutEffect fires synchronously before the browser paints — so DOM measurements and corrections happen invisibly. useEffect fires after paint, so any DOM change it makes can cause a visible flicker.
effect-vs-layouteffect.seeFlickerBody: Below, a box mounts at the left edge and an effect moves it to the right. With useEffect the browser can paint the box at the wrong spot first (a flash). With useLayoutEffect the move happens before paint, so you only ever see the final position. Switch modes and hit replay.
effect-vs-layouteffect.calloutBody: Default to useEffect — it doesn't block painting, so it keeps the UI responsive. Reach for useLayoutEffect only when you must read layout (size/position) and synchronously re-adjust the DOM to avoid a visible jump: tooltips, measuring, scroll restoration.

prop-drilling.title: Prop Drilling & Context
prop-drilling.tagline: Threading a prop through layers — and how Context skips them.
prop-drilling.intro: Prop drilling is what happens when a piece of data lives high in the tree but is only needed deep down. To get it there, you have to thread it as a prop through every component in between — even though those middle components don't use it at all. It works, but it makes the intermediate components noisy and awkward to refactor.
prop-drilling.watchDrilling: Watch the prop pass through every layer.
prop-drilling.watchContext: Context sends it straight to the consumer.
steps: App owns the user data
Passed down to Dashboard…
…which forwards it to Sidebar…
…which forwards it to ProfileMenu…
Finally Avatar receives and uses it
prop-drilling.callout1Title: When drilling is fine — and when it isn't
prop-drilling.callout1Body: Passing a prop down one or two levels is perfectly normal; don't reach for Context immediately. Drilling becomes a smell when the same prop travels through several components that only relay it, or when adding a new consumer means editing a long chain. Then a shared Context (or a state library) keeps the middle layers clean.
prop-drilling.callout2Body: Every component that calls useContext re-renders whenever the context value changes. For values that update very often, prefer splitting contexts, memoizing the value, or composing components (passing JSX as children) so you drill less without a global store.

hoc.title: Higher-Order Components
hoc.tagline: A function that wraps a component to add behavior.
hoc.intro: A Higher-Order Component is just a function that takes a component and returns a new one with extra behavior wrapped around it — the component equivalent of a decorator. The pattern: const Enhanced = withSomething(Base). React's own React.memo is an HOC, as were connect() (Redux) and withRouter.
hoc.wrapCaption: withLoading( ) — adds loading behavior + passes props through
hoc.inActionBody: ProfileWithLoading understands a new loading prop that plain Profile knows nothing about. Toggle it:
hoc.calloutBody: Most things HOCs were used for — sharing logic, injecting data, cross-cutting behavior — are now done more simply with custom hooks (e.g. a useLoading() hook instead of withLoading). HOCs are still worth knowing: you'll meet them in libraries, and React.memo is one you'll use constantly.
